subsystem: RawSpeed (librawspeed) camera-RAW decoder — the TableLookUp helper class. TableLookUp holds one or more 16-bit linearization / tone-curve lookup tables and TableLookUp::setTable(ntable, table) populates table slot number `ntable` from a caller-supplied vector of 16-bit values, in either a plain (non-dither) or a dithered layout. These tables are applied per-pixel while decompressing a RAW image to map raw sensor values to output values.
provenance: Identified from RawSpeed-specific identifiers: the ThrowRDE(...) error macro (Throw RawDecoderException), the ushort16 typedef (RawSpeed's alias for uint16_t), the TableLookUp class with its TABLE_SIZE constant, `tables`/`ntables`/`dither` members, and the setTable(int, const std::vector<ushort16>&) signature. The header comment `sample: primevul_train_2304_fixed` marks this as the post-fix revision of a PrimeVul training sample, so it is expected to be near-clean; it is reviewed blind on its merits.
trust_boundary: RawSpeed parses fully attacker-controlled camera RAW files. The lookup-table contents and the count of table entries (nfilled = table.size()) come from linearization/curve metadata inside the RAW file, and the table index `ntable` is likewise derived from file-controlled component/plane indices. Both `ntable` and the table vector are therefore hostile inputs. `tables`, `ntables`, `dither`, and TABLE_SIZE are trusted class state established elsewhere (constructor / allocation), but the safety of every write here depends on the bounds relationship between the attacker's `ntable`/nfilled and that trusted allocation.

what_the_code_does.code:
void TableLookUp::setTable(int ntable, const std::vector<ushort16>& table) {
  assert(!table.empty());
what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. `ntable` is the destination table slot to fill and `table` is a vector of 16-bit values (ushort16 = uint16_t) that describe the curve. The first statement is an assert that the input vector is non-empty. assert() is a debug-only guard: under NDEBUG (the normal release build configuration for a decoder library) it expands to nothing, so this line performs NO validation in production and merely documents an assumption that later code (which indexes table[nfilled-1]) relies on.

nfilled is the number of supplied table entries. table.size() returns a size_t (64-bit unsigned) which is narrowed into the signed `int` nfilled. The guard rejects tables with 65536 or more entries by throwing a RawDecoderException, which bounds nfilled to at most 65535 for the loops below and guarantees the per-entry input curve fits within the 65536-entry index space the tables are built over. This is the fix that keeps nfilled strictly below 65536.

The bounds check on the destination slot index. `ntables` is the class member holding how many tables were allocated. The intent is to reject an out-of-range slot before it is used to index into the `tables` buffer at line 12. It only checks the upper side (and, as discussed below, checks it with `>` rather than `>=`, and never checks the lower/negative side).

Computes the base pointer of the selected table slot inside the contiguous `tables` allocation. Each slot occupies TABLE_SIZE entries; TABLE_SIZE is 2*65536 (131072) in RawSpeed because the dithered layout stores two ushort16 (value + delta) per input index. `t` is the destination all subsequent writes target. This pointer's validity is entirely a function of the line-9 bounds check and the `ntable * TABLE_SIZE` arithmetic being correct.

The non-dither path. It fills the first 65536 entries of the slot: for each output index i it copies table[i] while i is within the supplied entries, otherwise it clamps by repeating the last supplied value table[nfilled-1]. The ternary's `i < nfilled` guard keeps the table[i] read in bounds; the fallback reads table[nfilled-1]. This writes t[0..65535] — the first half of the TABLE_SIZE slot — then returns, so the second half is untouched in this layout.

The dithered path, first loop, over the supplied entries. For each i it reads the current value (center), the previous (lower, clamped at the low edge), and the next (upper, clamped at the high edge). The neighbor reads are bounded: table[i-1] only when i>0 and table[i+1] only when i < nfilled-1, so all three indices stay in [0, nfilled). It then writes a pair per entry: t[i*2] gets a dither-adjusted base value and t[i*2+1] gets the delta between neighbors. With nfilled <= 65535, the largest index written here is (nfilled-1)*2+1 <= 131069.

The dithered path, tail loop, that fills the remaining output indices from nfilled up to 65535. Each pair is set to the clamped last value table[nfilled-1] with a zero delta. The largest index written is 65535*2+1 = 131071, i.e. the last entry of a 131072-entry (TABLE_SIZE) slot. Together the two dither loops write every entry t[0..131071] of the slot.

Endpoint fixups for the dithered layout: the first entry copies its neighbor and the very last entry copies the second-to-last, smoothing the boundaries of the delta scheme. Both accesses are within [0, TABLE_SIZE) and reachable only on the dither path (the non-dither path returned at line 17). They implicitly assert that TABLE_SIZE is at least 2 and that a full TABLE_SIZE-entry slot is allocated at `t`.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Off-by-one upper-bound check. Valid slot indices are 0..ntables-1 because `tables` holds exactly `ntables` slots of TABLE_SIZE entries. The guard uses `>` instead of `>=`, so ntable == ntables passes and line 12 computes t = &tables[ntables * TABLE_SIZE], which points exactly one full slot past the end of the allocation. The dither path then writes all TABLE_SIZE (131072) entries — up to 256 KiB — completely out of bounds, and the non-dither path writes 65536 out-of-bounds entries. This is a classic out-of-bounds write (CWE-787 / off-by-one CWE-193). The implementation must reject ntable >= ntables. Whether ntable can actually reach ntables depends on how the caller derives it from the RAW file, so this is a dangerous contract even if not directly exploitable in the shown snippet.

No lower-bound (negative) check on ntable. `ntable` is a signed int derived from file-controlled data and the only guard is the upper comparison. A negative ntable slips through, and line 12 computes &tables[ntable * TABLE_SIZE] with a negative offset, so `t` points before the allocation. Every subsequent store writes out of bounds behind the buffer — an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787, improper array-index validation CWE-129). The implementation must enforce 0 <= ntable < ntables. This must be confirmed against the caller: if any code path feeds an unvalidated signed index here, it is directly exploitable.

The only defense against an empty input table is an assert, which is compiled out under NDEBUG in release builds. If a RAW file yields an empty table, nfilled = 0 and the assert does nothing. Then the non-dither path executes t[i] = (i < 0) ? ... : table[nfilled - 1] = table[-1] for every i (line 15), and the dither tail loop reads table[nfilled - 1] = table[-1] (line 29): both are out-of-bounds reads of std::vector element index -1 (CWE-125), the underlying cause being reliance on an assertion for input validation of untrusted data (CWE-617). The implementation must reject an empty table with a real runtime check (ThrowRDE), not assert().

size_t-to-int narrowing. table.size() is a 64-bit unsigned value truncated into signed int nfilled. A table with more than INT_MAX (2^31-1) entries — or, on the wraparound, a size whose low 32 bits are large — truncates to a negative or unexpectedly small nfilled. A negative nfilled bypasses the `nfilled >= 65536` guard (line 6), after which table[nfilled - 1] indexes far out of bounds and the loops iterate incorrectly (CWE-197 numeric truncation feeding CWE-125 out-of-bounds read / CWE-190). Such a vector is ~4 GiB+, so this is a latent, low-probability defect, but the safe form computes and compares the size as an unsigned/size_t before narrowing.

Signed integer overflow in the offset arithmetic. ntable and TABLE_SIZE (131072) are both int; ntable * TABLE_SIZE overflows the 32-bit signed range once ntable exceeds ~16383. If `ntables` is ever large (allocation aside), a valid-looking ntable can produce a wrapped, negative, or otherwise wrong byte offset, yielding an out-of-bounds `t` and subsequent OOB writes (CWE-190 leading to CWE-787). The implementation must ensure ntable * TABLE_SIZE cannot overflow — e.g. compute the offset in size_t and bound ntables so the product stays in range. Latent; depends on the ntables value and allocation established in the constructor (file not provided).

The dither branch encodes an unstated allocation contract: it writes indices up to 65535*2+1 = 131071 and explicitly touches t[TABLE_SIZE-1]. This is only safe if every slot is allocated with TABLE_SIZE == 2*65536 entries. The non-dither branch, by contrast, only writes t[0..65535]. If TABLE_SIZE were defined as 65536 (single-value layout) while `dither` is true, the dither loops overflow the slot by a full 65536 entries — an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). The invariant the implementation must uphold is that `tables` is allocated as ntables * (2*65536) ushort16 whenever dither can be true. This is a latent contract to confirm in the constructor/allocation (file not provided).

summary: Post-fix RawSpeed TableLookUp::setTable. The entry-count fix (nfilled < 65536) is present and the in-loop table reads are properly clamped, but the destination-slot validation is weak: the ntable bounds check is off-by-one (`>` instead of `>=`) and has no negative-side check, so a file-controlled slot index can drive out-of-bounds writes of up to a full table past (or before) the `tables` allocation. Secondary latent issues: empty-table protection relies on a release-stripped assert (OOB read), a size_t->int truncation can bypass the entry-count guard, and the dither writes assume a 2*65536 slot allocation.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: void TableLookUp::setTable(int ntable, const std::vector<ushort16>& table)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Both parameters are attacker-influenced: ntable is an unvalidated signed slot index and table carries file-controlled contents/size.
Debug-only guard; stripped under NDEBUG, leaving empty-table input to trigger table[-1] out-of-bounds reads.
size_t-to-signed-int narrowing; a huge size truncates and can bypass the entry-count guard.
Correct upper-bound rejection of the entry count; keeps nfilled <= 65535 for all subsequent index math (only weakness is the truncation feeding it, tracked separately).
Off-by-one (`>` vs `>=`) and no lower-bound check on the destination slot index.
Trusted count of allocated slots; the off-by-one comparison against it is exactly the OOB-write vector.
All writes target this allocation; its size (ntables * TABLE_SIZE) is the invariant every finding tests against.
Signed int multiplication can overflow for large ntable, producing a wrong base pointer.
Base pointer whose validity depends entirely on the flawed ntable checks and the overflow-prone arithmetic.
Dither writes assume TABLE_SIZE == 2*65536 per slot; a mismatch overflows the slot.
Boolean that selects layout; carries no direct memory contract beyond the TABLE_SIZE assumption already captured.
Writes only t[0..65535], the first half of a TABLE_SIZE slot; in bounds given a valid base pointer. table[i]/table[nfilled-1] reads are clamped except in the empty-table case tracked at line 3.
Neighbor indices are guarded (i>0, i<nfilled-1, i<nfilled) so stay within [0,nfilled); the only unguarded read is table[nfilled-1] under empty/truncated input, tracked at lines 3 and 5.
Reach index up to 131071; safe only under the 2*65536 slot-allocation contract.
Possible negative-to-unsigned wrap is a data-correctness (curve) issue, not a memory-safety one.
Indices within [0,TABLE_SIZE); safe under the same allocation contract as the dither loops.
RawSpeed error/exception mechanism; the rejection path itself carries no memory contract.
Alias for uint16_t; the element width is consistent between vector, buffer, and writes.

findings.title: Off-by-one destination-slot bounds check permits full-table out-of-bounds write
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0007_cwe787.cpp (line 9); allocation of `tables` in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The destination slot index must satisfy 0 <= ntable < ntables, since `tables` holds exactly ntables slots of TABLE_SIZE entries.
findings.failure_mode: With `>` instead of `>=`, ntable == ntables passes; t = &tables[ntables*TABLE_SIZE] points one full slot past the end, and the dither path writes all 131072 entries (~256 KiB) out of bounds while the non-dither path writes 65536 entries out of bounds. If a crafted RAW file can set ntable to ntables, this is a remote heap out-of-bounds write.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the check is `ntable >= ntables` (not `>`), and trace how the caller derives ntable from file data to see whether ntable == ntables is reachable.

findings.title: Missing negative-index check on ntable allows write before the buffer
findings.invariant: ntable is a signed int and must be validated on the lower side (ntable >= 0) as well as the upper side before use as an offset.
findings.failure_mode: A negative, file-derived ntable passes the sole `> ntables` guard; &tables[ntable*TABLE_SIZE] then points before the allocation and every subsequent store writes out of bounds behind the buffer, corrupting adjacent heap memory.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm a lower-bound guard exists (either here or at every caller) and that ntable cannot be negative when it reaches this function.

findings.title: Empty-table validation relies on an assert stripped in release builds (OOB read of table[-1])
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via reliance on a compiled-out assertion
findings.invariant: An empty input table must be rejected with a real runtime check (ThrowRDE) before any code indexes table[nfilled-1]; assert() is not a runtime guard under NDEBUG.
findings.failure_mode: Under NDEBUG the assert vanishes. An empty table gives nfilled = 0, so the non-dither loop evaluates table[nfilled-1] = table[-1] for every iteration (line 15) and the dither tail loop reads table[-1] (line 29) — out-of-bounds reads of std::vector index -1 from an attacker-triggerable input.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is a non-assert runtime rejection of empty tables reachable in release builds before nfilled-1 is used.

findings.title: size_t-to-int truncation of table.size() can bypass the entry-count guard
findings.bug_class: Numeric truncation feeding an out-of-bounds read
findings.invariant: The entry count must be compared as an unsigned/size_t value; narrowing to signed int before the range check must not be able to change its sign or magnitude.
findings.failure_mode: table.size() (64-bit unsigned) is narrowed into signed int. A vector whose size exceeds INT_MAX truncates to a negative or small nfilled, which slips past `nfilled >= 65536`; subsequent table[nfilled-1] indexing and loop bounds then misbehave (OOB read / wrong writes). Requires a ~4 GiB+ vector, so latent/low.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the size is validated in size_t space (e.g. table.size() >= 65536) before being narrowed to int.

findings.title: Signed overflow in ntable * TABLE_SIZE offset arithmetic
findings.bug_class: Signed integer overflow producing a wrong base pointer
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0007_cwe787.cpp (line 12); ntables/allocation in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: ntable * TABLE_SIZE must be computed without overflow (e.g. in size_t) and must land within the ntables*TABLE_SIZE allocation.
findings.failure_mode: Both operands are int and TABLE_SIZE is 131072, so ntable > ~16383 overflows 32-bit signed math, yielding a wrapped/negative offset and an out-of-bounds `t`. Reachable only if ntables can be large, so latent and dependent on the constructor.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm ntables is small enough (or the offset is computed in size_t) that ntable * TABLE_SIZE cannot overflow int.

findings.title: Dither writes assume a 2*65536-entry slot allocation (latent OOB write)
findings.anchor: t[TABLE_SIZE - 1] = t[TABLE_SIZE - 2];  and dither loops writing t[i*2+1]
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write if the per-slot allocation is smaller than 2*65536
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0007_cwe787.cpp (lines 24-33); allocation of `tables` in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Whenever `dither` can be true, every slot must be allocated with TABLE_SIZE == 2*65536 ushort16 entries; the non-dither path uses only the first 65536.
findings.failure_mode: The dither branch writes up to index 131071 and touches t[TABLE_SIZE-1]. If TABLE_SIZE / the allocation were sized for the single-value layout (65536) while dither is true, these writes overflow each slot by 65536 entries. Depends on constructor definitions, hence latent.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the constructor allocates `tables` as ntables * (2*65536) ushort16 and that TABLE_SIZE equals 2*65536 whenever dither is enabled.

audit_checklist: Change the slot guard to reject ntable >= ntables (fix the off-by-one) and add ntable < 0 rejection; verify no caller can pass ntable == ntables or a negative index from file data.
Replace assert(!table.empty()) with a runtime ThrowRDE on empty input so release (NDEBUG) builds cannot reach table[-1].
Compare table.size() against 65536 in size_t before narrowing to int, to prevent truncation from bypassing the entry-count guard.
Compute the slot offset (ntable * TABLE_SIZE) in size_t and bound ntables so the product cannot overflow.
Confirm `tables` is allocated as ntables * (2*65536) ushort16 and TABLE_SIZE == 2*65536, matching the maximum index (131071) written by the dither path.
Verify that the values fed into ntable and the table vector originate from RAW-file parsing and are treated as untrusted throughout.
